name: Cova dels Ossos / s'Ullerol dets Ossos
starting_point: Road Ma-4042 km 6.350, junction with the Capdepera road and Camí de s'Heretat. Enter the forest toward the telephone line post; the cavity is to the left of it.
description: A small but charming previously uncatalogued cave discovered by Gaspar Herráez. Despite its compact size, it features soda straws, small flags, cow-udder formations, and flowstones. Notable for its fauna, including possibly Europe's smallest bat (Pipistrellus pipistrellus) and abundant animal bones that gave the cave its name. Considered of historical-archaeological and speleological interest.
full_description:
Despite its small dimensions, the Cova dels Ossos (Bones), so 'baptized' by me, is a charming cave that was uncatalogued until a week ago when Gaspar Herráez found it by chance, as often happens. Once he communicated the discovery to me, we made the first superficial exploration on November 17, 2007, leaving a more in-depth visit pending.

The entrance through which we will access its interior is of small dimensions, triangular in shape, approximately one meter high by about 65 or 70 centimeters at the widest part, and is oriented at 330° N.

Right after crossing the entrance, we will begin to descend down a small ramp of approximately 5 meters in length by a couple of meters at the widest side, but where we will move almost crawling along the ground due to the low ceiling. Once in the chamber where the small entrance gallery leads, we will immediately notice that it is of small dimensions — no longer than the entrance gallery, by about 4.5 m wide and about 3 m high, but only in one spot, since in the rest of the chamber we will always move crouching, unable to stand upright except at the indicated point. A tight crawlway opens before us to our right in the direction of the cave entrance, steeply sloped, with a length of approximately another 5 m, which in turn leads to another small chamber of a length equal to that of the crawlway and a maximum height of 1.90 m, ending the cave at this point.

Returning to the chamber to continue our visit, two possible options open before us: the first is through a crawlway on the right side, not suitable for certain body sizes, and the second is a kind of crack on the left side that, in a more or less comfortable way, will place us in another chamber of about 10 meters in length that also allows us to stand up while contemplating the formations found at the beginning of it — soda straws, small flags, cow-udder formations, as well as some flowstones can be seen in the place, all of small dimensions in keeping with the cavity we are in. At the back of the chamber, two new crawlways appear before us, one on each side, that descend for a few meters into the depths of the earth while blocking our passage, bringing the visit to a close.

I must highlight the fauna found in this small cavity which, despite its dimensions, is well worth noting. On my first visit, I was able to find a specimen of bat of very small dimensions never before seen by me, and once 'identified,' everything seems to indicate it was a Pipistrellus pipistrellus, which is the smallest bat that can be found in Europe. To give an idea of its dimensions, the forearm measures between 28 and 33.5 mm, where the hairs are blackish at the base and chestnut-colored at the tips, with short and small ears. The diet of this species consists mainly of flying insects and mosquitoes. I should also note an abundant number of black, shiny spiders — both the cephalothorax and the limbs — with the rest of the body being a dull brownish color. I also found a specimen of Scutigera coleoptrata, which is nothing more than a species of centipede but with somewhat fewer legs.

We did not have much trouble naming the cave, as there is a large quantity of bones from different animals in its interior, mainly in the entrance chamber as well as in the first described crawlway, which simplified the task.

The Cova dels Ossos is considered of historical-archaeological as well as speleological interest.

To learn more:

Jordi SERRA-COBO, Blanca AMENGUAL, Marc LÓPEZ-ROIG, Josep MÁRQUEZ, Xavier BAYER, Cisco GUASCH, Antonia SÁNCHEZ, Joan Antoni OLIVER. FIFTEEN YEARS OF CHIROPTEROLOGICAL STUDIES IN THE BALEARIC ISLANDS (1993-2007). Endins 31, 125-140. Palma de Mallorca.
